name: Briefing Note: Structure
description: Proposes a briefing note structure using APS conventions, tailored to the specific audience and purpose.
prompt_template:
<role>
You are a senior APS policy analyst preparing a briefing note for {{audience}} in {{agency}}.
</role>

<task>
Continuing from the research and key issues we've just developed, now propose a structure for a {{length}} briefing note on: {{topic}}

Audience: {{audience}}
Purpose: {{purpose}}

From the issues list above, pay particular attention to anything flagged as a sensitivity or information gap — these will shape where the brief needs to be most careful.

Use standard APS briefing note conventions. Typical sections include:
- Purpose (1–2 sentences: why this brief exists)
- Background (concise context the reader needs)
- Key issues / Analysis (the substance of the brief)
- Options or Recommendations (if the purpose calls for a decision)
- Next steps / Action required

Adapt the structure to suit this specific audience and purpose. For each proposed section:
- Provide the section heading
- Write 2–3 sentences on what it will cover and why it matters
- Note the approximate word count or length

Keep the total proposed brief within {{length}}.
</task>

variables.name: topic
variables.description: The policy topic for the briefing note

variables.name: audience
variables.description: Who will read this briefing note

variables.name: length
variables.description: Target length of the briefing note

beginner.notes: Skip this step if you're in a hurry — jump straight from research to drafting. The structure step is most useful when you need to present a complex topic that requires a non-standard layout.

intermediate.notes: Confirm the proposed structure before drafting. Adjust headings or emphasis based on what you know about your reader's preferences.

advanced.notes: Use the structure step to get explicit sign-off from your supervisor on the approach before investing time in a full draft.

known_limitations: Structure suggestions follow general APS conventions — your agency may have a specific template that takes precedence.
The model doesn't know your reader's personal preferences or communication style.
